title: 第 20 節
en: is loved by all men, or who grants their wishes to all men ; yet I do not think it is by accident that Kama, love, is thus applied to the rising sun.

Nor was the original solar character of the god of Love, the beloved of the Dawn, for- 174 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY gotten even in the later traditions of the FuranSiS. For we find that one of the names given to the son of Kama, to Aniruddha, the irresistible {dvLKaros /xaxav), is Ushapati, the lord of the Dawn.

If we place clearly before our mind all the ideas and allusions which have clustered round the name of Arvat and Arusha in the Veda, the various myths told of Eros, which at first seem so contradictory, become perfectly intelligible. He is in Hesiod the oldest of the gods, born when there is as yet only Chaos and Earth. Here we have days.' He is the youngest of the gods, the son of Zeus, the friend of the Charites, also the son of the chief Charis, Aphrodite, in whom we can hardly fail to discover a female Eros (an Usha instead of an Agni aushasya). Every one of these myths finds its key in the Veda. He is there ' the child, the son of Dyaus ; he yokes the Harits, and is, if not the son.i at least the beloved of the dawn.' Besides, in Greek mythology also, Eros has many fathers and many mothers ; and one pair of parents given him by Sappho, Heaven and Earth, is identical with his Vaidik parents, Dyaus and I^a. India, however, is not Greece, and though we may 1 Cf. Maxim, Tyr, XXIV., rbu "Epcord (p-qaiv i) Aiorifxa rc^ ZuKparei ov iraWa, dXX' aKoXovdov T-qs ' A(f)po5iTr}s Kai depdirovTa elvai. See Preller, Greek Mythology, p. 238.

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 175 trace the germs and roots of Greek words and Greek ideas to the rich soil of India, the full flower of Ar\'an language, of Ar^-an poetry and mythology, belongs to Hellas, where Plato has told us what Eros is, and where Sophokles sang his 'E/)a;s dvi/care fj.dx<^^, 'KpUiS, OS 4v KTTj/JLaffl TTtTTTetJ 5j ev fiaXaKah Trapeiah vfdvioos ej'vi'Xfi'f'S'

[Atttr'gone, 11, 775-790, ed. Wunder.] [O Love, 'gainst whom we fight in vain ;
O Love, who on thy \actims fall'st with might, And yet thou slumberest all night In the soft cheeks of maidenhood. Thou roamest far away beyond the seas. And dwellest at home in rustic cottages ;
None of the gods' immortal breed Nor yet of men shortlived Has e'er escaped from thee ;

And he whom thou dost seize Goes straightway mad.] ^ 1 [A curiously similar passage occurs in Steele's The Lazier, 1714 : " Among all the passions, to which the frailty and weakness of man subject him, there is not any that extends such a boundless and despolick Empire over the whole sj^ecies, as that (f Love. . . . This has subdued the exalted n.inds of the most aspiring tyrants, and has melted the most sanguine complexion into an effeminate softness. The mighty Hercules let fall his club at a woman's feet. The scholar, the statesman, and the soldier have all l)een lovers, and the most ignorant swain has neglected both his flocks and pipe to woo Daphne or Sylvia' (No. 25).] 176 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY If Hegel calls the discovery of the common origin of Greek and Sanskrit the discovery of a new world, the same may be said with regard to the common origin of Greek and Sanskrit mythology. The discovery is made, and the science of comparative mythology will soon rise to the same importance as comparative philology. I have here explained but a few myths, but they all belong to one small cycle, and many more names might have been added. I may refer those who take an interest in the geology of language to the Journal of Comparative Philology, published by my learned friend, Dr. Kuhn, at Berlin, who, in his periodical, has very properly admitted comparative mythology as an integral part of comparative philology, and who has himself discovered some striking parallelisms between the traditions of the Veda and the mythological names of the other Aryan nations. The very ' Hippokentaurs and the Chim^era, the Gorgons and Pegasos, and other monstrous creatures,' have been set right ; and though I do not hold Dr. Kuhn's views on many points, and particularly with regard to the elementary character of the gods, which he, like Lauer, the lamented author of the System of Greek Mythology, connects too exclusively with the fleeting phenomena of clouds, and storms, and thunder, while I believe their original conception to have been almost alwa3's solar.

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 177 yet there is much to be learnt from both, even where we cannot agree with their conclusions, ^luch, no doubt, remains to be done, and even wdth the assistance of the Veda, the whole of Greek mythology vdW never be deciphered and translated. But can this be urged as an objection ? There are many Greek words of which we cannot find a satisfactory etymology, even by the help of Sanskrit. Are we therefore to say that the whole Greek language has no etyanological organisation ? If we find a rational principle in the formation of but a small portion of Greek words, we are justified in inferring that the same principle which manifests itself in part governed the organic growth of the whole ; and though we cannot explain the etymological origin of all words, we should never say that language had no etymological origin, or that etymology ' treats of a past which was never present.' That the later Greeks, such as Homer and Hesiod, ignored the \6yos of their fj-vdoi I fully admit, but they equally ignored the real origin (76 (tvixov) of their words. What applies to etymology, therefore, applies with equal force to mythology. It has been proved by comparative philology that there is nothing irregular in language, and what was formerly considered as irregular in declension and conjugation is now recognised as the most regular and primitive formation of grammar. The same, we hope, may be M 178 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY accomplished in mythology, and instead of deriving it, as heretofore, ' ab ingenii humani imbecillitate et a dictionis egestate,' it will obtain its truer solution, ' ab ingenii humani sapientia et a dictionis abundantia.' IMyth ology is only a dialect, an ancient form of language. Mythology, though chiefly concerned with nature, and here again mostly with those manifestations which bear the character of law, order, power, and wisdom impressed on them, was applicable to all things. Nothing is excluded from mythological expression ; neither morals nor philosophy, neither history nor religion, have escaped the spell of that ancient sibyl. But mythology is neither philosophy, nor histor}', nor religion, nor ethics. It is, if we may use a scholastic expression, a quale, not a quid — something formal, not something substantial, and, Hke poetry, sculpture, and painting, applicable to nearly all that the ancient world could admire or adore.

zh: 是受到眾人喜愛，或是將眾人的願望賜予眾人；然而我不認為卡瑪（Kama）、愛，這樣應用於初升的太陽是出於偶然。
愛神——曙光的寵兒——原初的太陽性格，即使在後來的圖蘭傳統中也未曾被遺忘。我們發現，愛神迦摩（Kama）之子阿尼魯陀（Aniruddha）──那不可抗拒者（δυσμάχου μάχαν）──所被賦予的名號之一，即是烏莎帕蒂（Ushapati），曙光之主。

如果我們清晰地將環繞著吠陀中阿爾瓦特（Arvat）和阿魯沙（Arusha）名號的所有觀念與典故置於心中，那麼關於厄洛斯所講述的各種神話——乍看之下似乎彼此矛盾——就變得完全可以理解了。在赫西俄德的著作中，他是諸神中年代最久遠的，生於只有混沌與大地存在的時代。在這裡我們有「諸日」。他又是諸神中最年輕的，宙斯之子，美惠三女神的友人，同時也是主要美惠女神阿芙羅狄忒之子，而在她身上我們幾乎無法不發現一個女性厄洛斯（一個烏沙而非阿格尼‧奧夏薩亞）。這些神話中的每一個都在吠陀中找到了其解鑰。他在那裡是「幼兒，是特尤斯之子；他駕馭著哈利特，並且即使不是曙光之子，至少也是曙光所鍾愛之人」。此外，在希臘神話中，厄洛斯亦有許多父親與許多母親；薩芙所賦予他的一對親生父母——天與地——與其吠陀文獻傳統的父母特尤斯與伊達完全相同。然而印度並非希臘，儘管我們可能1 參見《馬克西慕斯‧泰魯斯》，第二十四卷，「厄洛斯」（希臘文原文）阿波羅尼奧斯說，『不是許多人所想的那樣，而是伴隨著阿芙羅狄忒且侍奉她的』。見普雷勒《希臘神話》，第二百三十八頁。
比較神話學 175 雖然可以追溯希臘詞彙和希臘觀念的萌芽與根源至印度這片肥沃的土壤——雅利安語言的盛花、雅利安詩歌和神話的豐碩成果——的完整展現，乃屬於赫拉斯。在那裡，柏拉圖曾向我們闡述厄洛斯（Eros）為何物，索福克勒斯唱誦其《厄洛斯》（Eros）：「愛啊，無敵者，你戰勝了柔軟的少女的肢體，你經歷了海洋……」
我準備好進行翻譯，但我注意到您提供的英文原文似乎不完整或出現亂碼： ``` <pOLTq.s 5' i'lrepTTOi'Tios, ev t' ``` 這段文字無法識別為有效的英文內容。請您提供完整清晰的英文原文，我將按照您列舉的規則忠實地翻譯成繁體中文。
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」實際上是古希臘文字符（ay povofiois avXais），而非英文。 請提供正確的英文原文，我將依照您的規則進行翻譯。

[Atttr'gone, 11, 775-790, ed. Wunder.] [啊愛啊，我等徒勞抗爭之對象；
啊，愛啊，你以強大之力降臨於你的犧牲者身上，然而你卻整夜沉睡在少女柔軟的臉龐裡。你遠遊於海外，也棲居於鄉野的茅舍之中；
無論是諸神之不朽種族 抑或短命的人類 皆未能逃脫汝之掌握；

而被汝所掌握者，輒時刻陷入瘋狂。]^ 1 [一段好奇地相似的段落出現在斯蒂爾（Steele）1714年的《閒散者》（The Lazier）中：「在人類脆弱與軟弱所使其陷入的眾多激情中，沒有任何一種能如愛那般，對整個人類物種施以如此無窮無盡且專制的統治……。此乃征服了最有野心的暴君們的崇高心靈，並使最旺盛的氣質消融為柔弱的溫和。偉大的海格力斯在一位女性的腳下放下了他的棍棒。學者、政治家與戰士皆曾為愛所役，最無知的鄉村小伙亦拋棄了他的羊群與笛子，以去追求達芙妮或西爾維亞》（第25期）。] 若黑格爾將希臘文與梵文共同起源的發現譽為一個新世界的發現，則對於希臘神話與梵文神話共同起源的發現亦可作同樣之言。發現業已告成，比較神話學之科學將迅速上升至與比較語言學等量齊觀之地位。我於此僅解釋了數則神話，然其皆屬於一個狹小的循環，尚有許多名字本可相加。我得以引介那些對於語言地質學感興趣者，參閱由我的博學友人庫恩博士（Dr. Kuhn）於柏林所出版之《比較語言學期刊》，此期刊甚為恰當地將比較神話學納為比較語言學之有機組成部分，而庫恩博士本人亦曾發現吠陀傳統與其他雅利安民族之神話名字之間數樁引人注目的類比。甚至「半人馬怪物（Hippokentaurs）與奇美拉（Chim√ra）、蛇髮女妖（Gorgons）與佩伽索斯（Pegasos），及其他怪物生物」，皆已獲得解釋；雖然我在許多點上與庫恩博士之見解不盡相同，尤其是關於諸神之根本性質，他與勞埃爾（Lauer，那位令人悲痛欲絕的《希臘神話體系》作者）一樣，過於專一地將之與雲、風暴及雷鳴等稍縱即逝的現象相連結，而我則信其原始觀念幾乎總是與太陽相關。

然而，即使我們無法同意他們的結論，我們仍然可以從兩者中學到許多東西。誠然，仍有許多工作有待完成，即使借助《吠陀》的幫助，整個希臘神話也永遠不會被完全解讀和譯解。但這能作為反對意見嗎？有許多希臘詞彙，即使借助梵語的幫助，我們也找不到令人滿意的詞源解釋。我們因此就該說整個希臘語言沒有詞源組織嗎？如果我們在希臘詞彙的一小部分形成中發現了理性原則，我們就有正當理由推斷，在部分中表現自身的同一原則也統轄著整體的有機發展；雖然我們無法解釋所有詞彙的詞源起源，但我們永遠不應該說語言沒有詞源起源，或說詞源學「處理的是從未存在過的過去」。我完全承認後期的希臘人，如荷馬和赫西俄德，忽視了他們*μῦθοι*的*λόγος*，但他們同樣也忽視了他們詞彙的真實起源（τὸ ἐτυμικόν）。因此，凡是適用於詞源學的，也同樣有力地適用於神話學。比較語文學已經證明了語言中沒有任何不規則之處，而從前被認為是不規則的屈折變化和動詞變位，現在被認可為最規則和最原始的文法形成。我們希望在神話學中也能成就同樣的事，而不是如往常一樣，「由於人類心智的軟弱與語言的匱乏」（*ab ingenii humani imbecillitate et a dictionis egestate*）來推導神話，它將獲得更真實的解決方案，「由於人類心智的智慧與語言的豐富」（*ab ingenii humani sapientia et a dictionis abundantia*）。神話學只是一種方言，一種古老的語言形式。神話學雖然主要關乎自然，在此又多半關乎那些帶有秩序、統一、力量與智慧特徵的現象，但它可應用於萬物。沒有任何事物被排除在神話表達之外；既非道德亦非哲學、既非歷史亦非宗教，都逃不過那位古老先知的咒語。然而，神話學既非哲學，亦非歷史，亦非宗教，亦非倫理。如果我們可以使用一個經院哲學的用語，它是一種*quale*（質），而非*quid*（本質）——是某種形式的東西，而非某種本質的東西，並且如同詩歌、雕刻與繪畫一樣，可應用於古代世界幾乎所有可敬拜或可崇敬的事物。
